question: Define abstraction in the context of Object-Oriented Programming and explain its primary purpose.
expectedPoints: Abstraction is the process of hiding implementation details and showing only essential features/functionality to the user.
It reduces complexity by allowing programmers to focus on interactions at a high level rather than internal workings.
In Java, abstraction is primarily achieved through abstract classes and interfaces.

question: Compare and contrast the use of an abstract class with an interface for achieving abstraction in Java. Provide a simple code example to illustrate each.
expectedPoints: An abstract class can have both abstract and concrete methods, while an interface (prior to Java 8) could only have abstract methods. Modern interfaces can have default and static methods.
A class can extend only one abstract class but can implement multiple interfaces.
Abstract classes are used for 'is-a' relationships and sharing code, while interfaces define a contract for 'can-do' relationships.
Code example for an abstract class: `abstract class Animal { abstract void makeSound(); }`
Code example for an interface: `interface Drivable { void drive(); }`

question: Design a system for a library using abstraction. Create an abstract class 'Item' and two concrete subclasses 'Book' and 'DVD'. Explain how abstraction is applied, and justify your design choices regarding the use of abstract methods versus concrete methods.
expectedPoints: The abstract class 'Item' would declare common properties (e.g., title, uniqueID) and abstract methods (e.g., `calculateLateFee(int daysLate)`) that enforce a contract for all library items.
Concrete subclasses 'Book' and 'DVD' provide specific implementations for the abstract methods, such as different algorithms for calculating late fees.
Abstraction is achieved by writing code that interacts with 'Item' references, allowing the system to handle any new type of item without modifying the existing code.

Justification: Abstract methods force subclasses to define specific behavior, ensuring polymorphism. Concrete methods in the abstract class can be used for shared functionality (e.g., `getTitle()`) to avoid code duplication.
The design promotes loose coupling and makes the system extensible for new item types in the future.
